system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — complete-course-vol-2

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

Mickey Baker's method is organized as a cumulative harmonic curriculum that moves from discrete voicing shapes toward integrated performance. Chapters 1 through 5 establish the vocabulary: Upper Partials as the governing harmonic concept, chord forms as the structural object of study, and the moving bass line and passing-tone voice leading as the first expressive tools. Chapters 6 through 11 shift from vocabulary to syntax, teaching the dominant-to-tonic cadence, chord cycle resolutions, relative-chord substitution, and forced harmony as the mechanisms by which chords become progressions. Chapters 12 through 19 introduce Symmetric Harmony and its associated devices—Symmetric Cycles, Augmented Cycles, Pedal Point, and the Cadence Break—as a parallel harmonic universe operating without a diatonic tonal center. Chapters 20 through 25 complete the edifice with chord analysis, diminished and augmented substitutions, and finally Counterpoint, which reframes everything previously learned as harmonic support for independent melodic lines. The final three chapters (27–29) contain no prose instruction; they are fully notated chord-melody solos by the author that function as comprehensive worked demonstrations of the entire method in performance context.

## Characteristic Systems and Terminology

Two master systems underlie the entire method. The first is **functional diatonic harmony** organized around the II–V–I resolution chain: the dominant (V) is the gravitational center of any tonality; the II chord prepares it; all elaborations—however many chords are inserted into a cycle—must eventually reach V and resolve to I (Chapter 9's governing rule). Chapter 8 establishes the I–IV–V–I and I–II–V–I progressions as memorization prerequisites before any substitution work begins. The second system is **Symmetric Harmony**, introduced in Chapter 13 as a key-signature-free framework in which every chord functions as a tonic and harmonic motion distributes equally across the 12-tone scale. Its practical notation is T1 through T4 (four equal divisions via minor thirds) or three divisions via major thirds for Augmented Cycles (Chapter 17), extending to six or twelve tonics by inserting intermediate chords. The diminished 7th chord is the emblematic vehicle of Symmetric Harmony: constructed from successive minor thirds, it maintains the same pitch classes across all inversions and positions, making every one of its tones a potential tonic. Chapter 26 explicitly names Symmetric Harmony and Counterpoint as the two principal theoretical systems the entire method rests on.

The load-bearing terminology, accumulated lesson by lesson, forms a precise lexicon: *Upper Partials* (the harmonic overtones that motivate modern voicing color); *chord forms* (voicing shapes distinct from rhythm chords); *moving bass line* (single-note melodic motion beneath static chords); *passing tones* (the voice-leading mechanism connecting ninth to sixth chords); *R* and *P* notation for Root and Passing tone in chord diagrams; *forced harmony* (adding chords via progressions that move away from and back to the tonic to keep music active); *Symmetric Cycle* (transposing any chord shape in minor thirds up or down the fingerboard); *Augmented Cycle* (transposing by major thirds); *Pedal Point* (sustaining a single tone—typically the fifth—while harmonies shift above it); *Cadence Break* (a one-to-two-bar structural pause at phrase endings); *Cantus Firmus* and *Counter melody* (the two independent voices of counterpoint); and *Rhythmic Chord and Melody Playing* (guitar as harmonic-melodic background in ensemble contexts).

## Rules and Prescriptions

The method establishes several explicit, actionable rules. When a voicing is physically awkward in its notated position, move it up the neck to a higher chord rather than force the fingering (Chapter 1). The fourth finger must be practiced chromatically across frets 1 through 10 to build the dexterity required for chord forms (Chapter 2). Beginners struggling with first-position five-fret stretches may begin in fourth or fifth position, with the expectation that facility arrives through practice rather than struggle (Chapter 4). All exercises must be analyzed thoroughly and then transposed chromatically up and down the entire fingerboard in both minor and major modes—this chromatic transposition drill is a standing practice rule, first stated in Chapter 5. Chapter 9 states the harmonic constraint governing all elaborations: no matter how many substitute or passing chords are added to a cycle, the progression must eventually reach the dominant and resolve to the tonic. Chapter 14 mandates retrograde practice—playing Symmetric Cycle exercises backwards from position 4 to position 1—to build descending facility. In chord-melody arranging, the melodic line takes priority over harmonic completeness: chords must be omitted or subordinated when they conflict with the melody (Chapter 26). Chapter 19 prescribes an absolute study method: exercises must be internalized through memorization and diligent repetition, never merely glanced at. Chapter 11 adds the complementary principle that after mastering all variations and committing them to memory, the student selects only those that align with personal musical taste—balancing technical command with artistic choice.

## Method and Methodology

The book's pedagogy is built on three interlocking methodological moves. The first is **dual-line contrast**: from Chapter 5 onward, standard progressions appear on one line and modern progressions on a second, allowing the student to hear the gap between traditional dominant-to-tonic resolution and the more colorful ninth-to-sixth voice leading the method teaches. The second is **cross-referenced voicing architecture**: circled numbers above chords refer back to the foundational voicing chart from Lesson 1, so every new exercise is explicitly anchored to previously learned material rather than introduced in isolation. The third is **systematic expansion of substitution privilege**: the method grants the student increasing license to deviate from written chord changes—Chapter 10 explicitly positions the player's ear and the "musical sounds that sound best to you" as the authority for substitutions, and asserts that jazz arrangements characteristically diverge from standard progressions—but this freedom is earned through the prior chapters' disciplined memorization of cycles, resolutions, and voicing forms.

The explicit performance methodology culminates in chord-melody playing (developed across Chapters 20–26), where arpeggiated bass voices carry independent melodic content, non-standard voicings are invented to fit the melodic line, and Counterpoint—two independent melodies moving simultaneously, one as Cantus Firmus and one as Counter melody—becomes the conceptual destination the entire harmonic study has been building toward. Ensemble application is handled in Chapters 23–24, where Pedal Point is distributed across players: the bass sustains the pedal tone, the guitar or piano executes harmonic variations above it, and a melody instrument carries the song's line. The three closing solos (Chapters 27–29) embody this synthesis, presenting Upper Partial voicings, symmetric cycles, and chord-melody writing as a unified performance language rather than separate technical modules.

# Chapter 1 — Chord Forms

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 introduces Upper Partials as the method's foundational concept for modern harmonic color in guitar voicings, establishing that students will study chord forms designed to exploit these overtones rather than traditional rhythm or comping chords. The chapter's scope is deliberately restricted: students learn harmonic vocabulary and voicing shapes while assuming a rhythmic foundation comes from other instruments, placing the focus entirely on upper-partial coloration and resonance. The master's characteristic move appears in the transposition advice—when a voicing proves difficult in its notated position, move it up the neck to a higher chord rather than forcing awkward fingering—a pragmatic principle that honors both the harmonic intent and the player's physical comfort. Load-bearing terminology here is twofold: Upper Partials names the harmonic resource the method explores, while chord forms (distinct from rhythm chords) identifies the specific object of study. Chapter 1 functions as pure vocabulary building; the movement and application of these forms—the actual harmonic narrative—begins in Lesson 2.

The chapter's pedagogical approach emphasizes systematic, chromatic practice to build muscle memory and internalize the relationship between finger position and chord voicing. This combination of sustained tones, mobile voices, and consistent bass motion creates the framework for understanding how chords can be transformed through position shifts rather than finger repositioning.

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches chord exercises that bridge Modern and Standard guitar playing techniques through a series of structured drills. The core technical concept is the moving bass line—a single-note progression played underneath a static chord—which creates a counterpoint texture that may appear complex in notation but becomes straightforward once understood as independent melodic motion below harmonic anchors. By working through these exercises, players develop the ability to voice chords while maintaining a flowing bass line, a skill that distinguishes advanced playing from beginner technique. The chapter's characteristic move is the pedagogical patience of acknowledging notation strangeness upfront, explaining that the apparent complexity resolves once the moving line is heard as melodically separate from the chord. This lesson represents a deliberate closure of the technical gap between Modern and Standard approaches, making it foundational for balanced guitar facility. The load-bearing terms introduced are "moving bass line" and the implicit distinction between harmonic (chord) and melodic (bass) layers that coexist in the same passage.

# Chapter 4 — Simple Chord Analysis

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches simple chord analysis through position-aware exercises that move from minor ninth to minor sixth chords and major ninth to major sixth chords, all via passing tones, establishing harmonic motion as the conceptual framework for both minor and major modes. The master's characteristic move is pragmatic scaffolding: while exercises begin in first position requiring a full five-fret stretch, students who find this difficult are permitted to start in fourth or fifth position, acknowledging that eventual facility comes through practice rather than forced struggle. The chapter introduces essential load-bearing terminology—the chord pair types (minor ninth, minor sixth, major ninth, major sixth), the mechanism of resolution (passing tones), and the notational legend ("R" for Root, "P" for Passing tone) that governs how every chord diagram on the page must be read. By placing major and minor forms side by side and noting that mode is their sole structural difference, the chapter reinforces harmonic parallelism as a core organizing principle.

# Chapter 8 — Resolutions of Chord Cycles

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 teaches the foundational concept of chord cycle resolutions, establishing that the dominant V chord demands resolution into the tonic I chord, whose arrival satisfies the ear and completes harmonic closure. The master's characteristic move is to organize all harmonic motion hierarchically around the dominant as the most active chord in a tonality, showing how any intermediate chord in a cycle eventually gravitates toward V before resolving to I. The chapter introduces and prioritizes two essential cycles: the I-IV-V-I progression as a basic model, and the I-II-V-I cycle as the primary focus, framing the latter as preparation for the substitution techniques to follow. Key terminology includes the tension-and-release dynamic of V→I resolution, the concept of "temporary resolution" for intermediate chords, and the recognition of the dominant as the harmonic center of gravitational pull. The chapter emphasizes memorization of these cycles as a prerequisite for understanding the minor II substitution that lesson 9 will introduce, making this chapter the essential theoretical foundation for advanced harmonic substitution work.

# Chapter 9 — Related Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 teaches the concept of relative chords—chords that share identical tones or where one chord's tones form a subset of another's (such as C6 and Am7, or Dm as the upper three tones of G9)—and applies this principle to chord substitution within progressions. The core technique demonstrates how to insert the II chord before the V chord in a I-V-I progression, transforming a simple four-bar skeleton (one bar I, two bars V, one bar I) into four elaborated variants that add intermediate chords while preserving the underlying harmonic motion. The master's characteristic move is methodical expansion: each of the four examples in Example 22 adds more chords to the cycle, yet all maintain the fundamental I-V-I shape, showing how substitution creates complexity without obscuring underlying structure. The load-bearing rule states that no matter how many chords are added to elaborate a cycle, they must eventually reach the dominant and resolve into the tonic, establishing the constraint that governs all permissible elaborations. This chapter introduces the concept of relative chords and the II-V substitution as foundational tools for reharmonizing simple progressions.

# Chapter 10 — Altered Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 introduces Forced Harmony—a compositional technique that adds chords via progressions moving away from and back to the tonic, keeping music active rather than static—and establishes the Upper Partials as the load-bearing concept for chord alteration: the dominant can be extended to 7th, 9th, 11th, augmented 11th, or 13th, with the 5th or 9th potentially flatted, all resolving to the tonic; the II chord follows the same principle with its own Upper Partials (6th, 7th, 9th, 11th, 13th) resolving through the V to the tonic, forming the jazz II-V-I resolution chain. The master's characteristic move is privileging player judgment and arranging creativity over literal adherence to original chord progressions, positioning the student's ear and "musical sounds that sound best to you" as the authority for substitutions and alterations. The chapter asserts that II can substitute for V and that jazz arrangements characteristically diverge from standard written progressions, granting the soloist explicit license to rebuild harmony through extended chords and harmonic movement rather than playing changes as written.

# Chapter 12 — The Cadence

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch12.md`._

Chapter 12 teaches the Authentic Cadence, the harmonic gesture that ends a musical thought by moving from the dominant (V) chord to the tonic (I), establishing closure and tonality. The load-bearing terminology is "Authentic Cadence" itself, which names this V-I progression and its functional role in harmonic syntax. The master emphasizes that cadential thinking applies at any structural scale—not only in standard phrase lengths but in single- or two-bar sequences—making the Authentic Cadence a scalable principle rather than a fixed form.

# Chapter 13 — Symetric Harmony

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch13.md`._

Symmetric Harmony is a key-signature-free system in which chords progress freely without the hierarchical tonal center of diatonic harmony, introduced at cadences and structural breaks where the tonic chord anchors endings. The chapter's core principle states that in Symmetric Harmony every chord functions as a tonic, with practitioners selecting any number of tonics; the practical framework divides the 12-tone scale into four equal parts using a T1-through-T4 notation system where each chord is labeled with a "T" and number to indicate which tonic it represents. The master technique centers on the diminished 7th chord, which moves freely up and down the fingerboard in minor thirds, maintaining the same tones across positions but in different arrangements. Because a diminished chord is constructed from successive minor thirds, each tone within it serves as a potential root or tonic, making the chord enharmonically equivalent regardless of inversion or position—a property demonstrated in Exercise 31b. This system exemplifies the characteristic move of treating harmonic movement as a non-linear, symmetrically distributed space rather than a tonally centered journey.

# Chapter 14 — Symetric Cycles

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch14.md`._

Chapter 14 introduces Symmetric Cycles, a technique for transposing chord shapes by moving them up or down the fretboard in ascending or descending minor thirds, with only the first chord diagrammed since the remaining positions follow the same shape at the higher intervals. The chapter presents four written examples and establishes a characteristic left-hand move where the fourth finger transfers from the 2nd to the 3rd string as the player progresses up the fretboard in these minor-third increments. The master emphasizes that any chord can be played as a Symmetric Cycle, whether ascending from a low position or descending from a high position, making this a universal transposition tool rather than a context-specific technique. A practical constraint acknowledges that cutaway guitars allow extension to higher positions while non-cutaway instruments are limited by their body design. The chapter includes a retrograde practice rule directing students to play the exercises backwards (from position 4 back to 1) to build facility with descending motion, cementing "Symmetric Cycle" and "minor third" as the load-bearing terminology for this transposition vocabulary.

The load-bearing terminology centers on the Symmetric Cycle itself, minor thirds as the intervallic vehicle for the motion, and the concept of chord fragments as viable alternatives to complete voicings.

# Chapter 17 — Augmented Cycles

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch17.md`._

Chapter 17 teaches augmented chord construction based on major thirds and their symmetric properties across the chromatic scale. An augmented chord (such as C–E–G#) maintains its pitch-class identity when transposed up a major third, enabling the formation of an "Augmented Cycle" by dividing the 12-tone scale into three equal parts to yield three distinct tonic chords, a principle demonstrated in Exercises 38–40. The chapter expands this framework to accommodate six tonics by inserting intermediate chords into the cycle (Exercise 41), and extends further to introduce "Symmetric Harmony," a technique in which parallel voice leading connects start and end harmonic points regardless of intervening motion, permitting up to 12 tonics within a single progression (Exercise 42). The master's characteristic approach treats harmonic motion as a matter of endpoints and direction rather than voice-leading detail, emphasizing the mathematical economy of equal division and transposition as organizing principles for harmonic exploration.

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch21.md`._

# Chapter 22 — The Cadence Break

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch22.md`._

The Cadence Break is a structural pause occurring at the end of musical thoughts where the music relaxes for one to two bars before repeating or progressing to new material, and it can be filled with Symmetric Cycles, Pedal Point, or original chord progressions to add color and originality. Pedal Point, the chapter's cornerstone technique, sustains a single note—typically the fifth of the scale—across multiple measures while varying chords play overhead, with the most popular form in C Major using a G pedal supporting Dm9 and G13b9 chords. The master demonstrates characteristic resourcefulness by showing how to adapt Pedal Point across keys through strategic retuning: lowering the 6th string a whole tone to D for G Major, lowering the 5th string a whole tone to G for C Major in half-note rhythm, and raising the 5th string a half tone to Bb for Eb Major. The chapter introduces flexibility in rhythm by specifying that the pedal note can be articulated as whole notes, half notes, quarter notes, or eighth notes, giving players multiple interpretive choices within the same harmonic framework. Load-bearing terms include "Cadence Break" (the structural pause), "Pedal Point" (the sustained-note technique), and "half-note Pedal Point" (a rhythmic variant), which together form the vocabulary for understanding how to create introductions and transitions with harmonic sophistication.

# Chapter 25 — Counterpoint

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch25.md`._

Counterpoint is the movement of two or more independent melodies simultaneously rather than the use of chord harmony, representing an essential technique in modern music that derives from classical tradition and particularly benefits small ensemble playing. The fundamental rule is that when two lines move against one another, one melody can sustain itself for several beats while the other line moves in faster note values, creating rhythmic contrast and textural variety. The technical terminology labels these voices as the Cantus Firmus (the original melody) and the Counter melody (the added melody), though simpler names suffice in practice. The master's characteristic approach prioritizes harmonic integrity above all—the examples avoid bad harmonic movement and move chordally from one direction to another without lingering excessively on single notes, ensuring that melodic independence never compromises the underlying harmonic foundation. A key technique is the sustaining counter melody, which extracts only the first note from each chord to create a strong, straight-line bass quality while the primary melody dances freely above it, as demonstrated in the progression from Exercise 49 to Exercise 65.

> the cycle in which we are most interested is from dominant V to tonic I. Now

## Dominant as most active chord

> This is the best way to use the guitar when working
> with a small combo such as bass, drums and guitar, or other small combinations. Once

> today many groups work without the piano.

> Practice the following exercises until they are part of you. Be absolute in your study.

## Cadence Break definition

> the Cadence Break occurs at the end of a musical
> thought. Looking through any popular sheet music, you will see that at certain designated
> points the music relaxes for one or two bars and then either repeats itself or progresses
> into another musical line. These points are known as the Cadence Breaks

**Significance (p. 36).** Foundational definition of the Cadence Break concept.

## Cadence Break duration examples

> Notice that at this Cadence Break the music relaxes on one note for 1 and 3/4 bars.
> In exercise 55 it relaxes for 1 and 1/4 bars.

**Significance (p. 36).** Specifies precise rhythmic durations of Cadence Breaks.

## Harmonizing the Cadence Break

> Semetric Cycles, Peda] Point and other types of original chord progressions may be em-
> ployed at these points. The musical thought has ended. Any new chord progressions that
> may be added give new color and originality.

**Significance (p. 36).** Rule for how to fill a Cadence Break — Symmetric Cycles, Pedal Point, or new progressions.

## Pedal Point definition

> A Pedal Point is a sustained or pedal note held or sustained for several measures
> while a variety of chords are introduced. The most popular note in Pedal Point is the fifth
> of the scale.

**Significance (p. 37).** Core definition of Pedal Point including the rule that the fifth of the scale is the canonical pedal.

## Pedal Point in C Major (Ex. 56)

> In exercise 56 there is an introduction to the key of C Major, the pedal note
> is G. While the pedal note is being sustained, a Dm9 and a G13b9 chord play. This is the
> most popular form of Pedal Point.

**Significance (p. 37).** Names specific chords used over a G pedal in C Major.

## Retuning 6th string for Pedal Point

> Exercise 57 is an introduction which is to be used in the key of G Major.
> Lower the 6th stringon your guitar a whole toneto D. Practice this exercise until you are
> thoroughly familiar with the idea and sound of Pedal Point.

**Significance (p. 37).** Specific retuning instruction (6th string down a whole tone to D).

## Half-note Pedal Point, 5th string retuning

> In exercise 58, you lower the 5th string a whole tone to G. This is an
> introduction tothe key of C Major in half note Pedal Point, which fits perfectly to the mu-
> sical exercise "I Dream of You".

**Significance (p. 37).** Second retuning variant (5th string to G), introduces half-note Pedal Point rhythm.

## Pedal Point variation, Eb key

> In exercise 59 you raise your 5th string up half a tone to Bb. Here you have an introduc-
> tion in the key of Eb using still another variation of Pedal Point.

**Significance (p. 37).** Third retuning instruction (5th string up a half tone to Bb) extends Pedal Point to a flat key.

## Pedal Point note-value options

> There are many ways to play Pedal Point, such as whole notes, half notes, quarter notes
> and eighth notes.

**Significance (p. 37).** Rule enumerating all rhythmic subdivisions available for the pedal note.

_Pages 41-41._

## Counterpoint definition

> Counterpoint is, in reality, the movement of two or more melodies rather than the
> use of chord harmony. It plays a very important part in modern music even though it ts
> from the "Old School". Some small combos derive great benefit from the use of it.

**Significance (p. 41).** Foundational definition of counterpoint as melodic movement rather than chordal harmony.

## Two-line movement rule

> When two lines move against one another that is a form of Counterpoint. There are times
> when one melody sustains itself for as many as 4 beats, while the other line moves along
> in quarter or eighth notes.

**Significance (p. 41).** Operative rule for recognizing counterpoint and introduces sustained vs. moving line rhythmic contrast.

## Cantus Firmus / Counter melody terminology

> There are many names for these melodies: Cantus Firmus, meaning the original melody;
> Counterpoint, meaning the added melody, sometimes referred to as the Counter melody.
> However, these are technical terms and melody and counter melody will suffice.

**Significance (p. 41).** Defines the two technical terms labeling each voice.

## Harmonic movement standard

> Most of the examples in this book are brief and move chordally from one direction to an-
> other. Though they never linger on one note for any length of time, there is never a sign
> or sound of bad harmonic movement.

**Significance (p. 41).** Articulates the book's governing aesthetic rule — avoid bad harmonic movement regardless of melodic independence.

## Sustaining counter melody technique

> Exercise 65 illustrates a melody and counter melody moving along simultaneously. This
> is the same example in exercise 49 of lesson 19. The difference is that only the first note
> in each chord of the counter melody is used. This ts a sustaining type of counter melody
> and it offers a strong straight line quality while the melody dances along on top of it.

**Significance (p. 41).** Describes the technique of extracting only the first note from each chord to create a sustained bass-line counter melody.
